coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot and DNA tetrahedron nanostructure based electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for AFB1 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ying
Song, Pengyue
Xu, Qingbin
Wu, Wenjuan
Long, Nan
Wang, Jiabo
Zhou, Lidong
Pan, Ruile
Kong, Weijun
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor with aptamer as recognition element, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as luminophore, and DNA tetrahedral nanostructure (DTN) as carrier to loading abundant N-CDs for signal amplification was developed for ultra-sensitive aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) detection. The complementary DNA (cDNA) was firstly immobilized on the Au electrode (AuE) surface to hybridize with the AFB1 aptamer. In the presence of AFB1, it would competitively with cDNA for binding the aptamer, causing the exposure of cDNA. DTN modified with AFB1 aptamer on top and N-CDs on the four vertices would combine with cDNA onto the AuE surface to generate ECL signals due to electron transfer between co-reactants (K2S2O8) and N-CDs, which was positively related to the concentration of AFB1. Due to the high specificity of aptamer, the outstanding ECL performance of N-CDs and the enhanced signal output of DTN/N-CDs probes, the as-constructed ECL aptasensor realized excellent sensitivity with a low detection limit of 77 pg/mL and a wide linear concentration of 1.0 × 10−1-1.0 × 104 ng/mL for AFB1, as well as fascinating accuracy with acceptable recoveries of 89.54–111.0% in the spiked Coix seed matrices. Successful application for AFB1 detection in actual samples confirmed the extensive practicability of the ECL aptasensor, providing a universal platform for more trace contaminants by altering the corresponding aptamers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence aptasensor
N-doped carbon dots
DNA tetrahedral nanostructure
AFB1
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of N-CDs and DTN based ECL aptasensor
ECL measurement of AFB1 in actual samples
Optimization of the fabrication conditions for the aptasensor
Analytical performance of the ECL sensors
Real application of the proposed ECL aptasensor for AFB1 detection

Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the support from the Beijing Natural Science Foundation ( 7222285 , 7232265 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82274089 , 81973474 ), and the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources ( SKLTCM2022022 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CDs (The inserted picture represented the distribution histogram of particle size); (B) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs; (C) Confirmation of the synthesis of DTNs by agarose gel electrophoresis. 1, marker; 2, strand 1; 3–7: parallel experiments for DTN.

EIS (A) and ECL (B) characterization of the step-by-step construction of the ECL aptasensor. EIS was carried out in 0.01 M PBS solution containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and 0.4 M KNO3, and ECL was carried out in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) solution containing 0.1 M K2S2O8.
Optimization of the (A) concentration of cDNA incubated on the AuE, (B) incubation time of cDNA, and (C) pH of the co-reaction solution (in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0.1 M K2S2O8).

(A) ECL-time responses under the addition of various concentrations of AFB1 (from 1.0 ×10−1 ng/mL to 1.0 ×104 ng/mL); (B) Calibration curve for AFB1 determination (plot of I versus the logarithm of AFB1 concentration); (C) ECL responses (I) of the aptasensor upon incubation with AFB1 (1.0 ×103 ng/mL), and other three interfering mycotoxins (1.0 ×104 ng/mL); (D) Reproducibility of the ECL aptasensor; (E) ECL responses of the aptasensor within 7 days (that in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0.1 M K2S2O8).
Illustration of the synthesis of (A) DTN, N-CDs, and DTN/N-CDs, and (B) DTN/N-CDs based ECL aptasensor for AFB1 detection.
Comparison of the newly-developed ECL aptasensor with other reported methods for AFB1 detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: EC-Electrochemistry; FL-Fluorescence; PEC-photoelectrochemistry; GQDs-Graphene quantum dots; Fc-Ferrocene; MB-Methylene blue; QDs-Quantum dots; QBs-quantum dot nanobeads;

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of AFB1 from the spiked Coix seed samples.
AFB1 determination in actual Coix seed samples by using the developed ECL aptasensor.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot and DNA tetrahedron nanostructure based electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for AFB1 detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Capital Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor with aptamer as recognition element, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as luminophore, and DNA tetrahedral nanostructure (DTN) as carrier to loading abundant N-CDs for signal amplification was developed for ultra-sensitive aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) detection. The complementary DNA (cDNA) was firstly immobilized on the Au electrode (AuE) surface to hybridize with the AFB1 aptamer. In the presence of AFB1, it would competitively with cDNA for binding the aptamer, causing the exposure of cDNA. DTN modified with AFB1 aptamer on top and N-CDs on the four vertices would combine with cDNA onto the AuE surface to generate ECL signals due to electron transfer between co-reactants (K2S2O8) and N-CDs, which was positively related to the concentration of AFB1. Due to the high specificity of aptamer, the outstanding ECL performance of N-CDs and the enhanced signal output of DTN/N-CDs probes, the as-constructed ECL aptasensor realized excellent sensitivity with a low detection limit of 77 pg/mL and a wide linear concentration of 1.0 × 10−1-1.0 × 104 ng/mL for AFB1, as well as fascinating accuracy with acceptable recoveries of 89.54–111.0% in the spiked Coix seed matrices. Successful application for AFB1 detection in actual samples confirmed the extensive practicability of the ECL aptasensor, providing a universal platform for more trace contaminants by altering the corresponding aptamers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxin, which is exceedingly deleterious to human health, is a secondary toxic metabolite produced by certain fungi during the growth and reproduction processes [1]. They are carcinogenic, teratogenic, mutagenic, and immunosuppressive to pose serious harm to humans and animals even in trace amount. Of them, aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is the most poisonous mycotoxin in the aflatoxin family known to be a group 1 A carcinogen [2–4]. AFB1 can be frequently monitored in diverse foods, feeds and crops, and the AFB1 problem has always been of worldwide concern. Thus, it is crucial and urgent to develop sensitive and reliable methods for sensitive detection of AFB1.

Although conventional techniques including liquid chromatography coupled to different detectors with high sensitivity and accuracy have been constructed to address this issue, they require expensive instruments, professional operators, and high consumption, and are limited in professional laboratories [5–7]. As an ideal alternative, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensors, the perfect combination of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence, have recently obtained rapid development for trace small molecule analysis in complex matrices, exhibiting fascinating advantages regarding rapid response, low background, high sensitivity, simple equipment, low cost, and controllable luminescence reaction time and position [8–10]. In this sensing platform, target-specific aptamer was introduced as capture probe for the accurate recognition of diverse small molecules owing to its latent superiorities over conventional antibody, such as low production cost, fast synthesis, high affinity, and minimized differences between batches [1,11–13], which endowed the ECL aptasensors with outstanding specificity and wide application potential in different fields [14].

A highly-valuable ECL aptasensor needs not only aptamer as the identification unit, but also an excellent ECL luminophore [15]. Luminol, quantum dots (QDs) and some nanomaterials are traditionally utilized as ECL emitters [16–18], however, which either display high toxicity and cost, or are difficult to immobilize, or cannot satisfy the need for ultrasensitive detection. Thus, it is urgent and imperative to explore environmental-friendly and biocompatible ECL emitters with improved response intensity and co-reaction efficiency. As a brand new ECL luminophore, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit many strengths such as easy synthesis, nontoxicity, low cost, good water solubility, and favorable biocompatibility [19]. However, the ECL efficiency of conventional pure CDs is low due to their intrinsic electronic band structure. Then, the heteroatom-doped CDs, such as nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), have attracted many interests [20–22], which with more surface traps can accelerate electron transfer between co-reactants and N-CDs [23], displaying stronger ECL efficiency than pristine CDs.

For trace analysis of AFB1, signal amplification though enriching more N-CDs is necessary to improve the detection sensitivity of the ECL aptasensors. Then, DNA tetrahedron nanostructure (DTN), a kind of solid three-dimensional (3D) pyramid-like structure self-assembled by annealing of four complementary DNA single strands, has attracted remarkable attention in biosensing application owing to its simple synthesis, high programmability and structural rigidity [24–26]. Its rigid 3D scaffold is beneficial for loading abundant probe molecules like N-CDs and enhancing diverse signal amplification reactions, exhibiting the reduced hybridization steric hindrance and non-specific adsorption at the surface [27] to effectively improve the ECL intensity. However, it has not been introduced in fabricating N-CDs based ECL aptasensors.

Herein, for the first time, a simple N-CDs and DTN based competitive-type ECL aptasensor was developed for AFB1 detection ( Scheme 1). The blue-emissive N-CDs with excellent ECL responses were synthesized with citric acid and urea as precursor molecules by using the hydrothermal method. DTN was synthesized by one-step annealing method and was combined with N-CDs through amide bond to obtain DTN/N-CDs complex as the signal probe. Then, the complementary DNA (cDNA) against AFB1 aptamer was immobilized on the Au electrode (AuE) surface firstly to hybridize with the aptamer to form double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). Subsequently, the competitive binding of target AFB1 with its specific aptamer led to the formation of AFB1-aptamer complex, which was afterwards dissociated from the electrode, resulting in the exposure of cDNA again. Finally, the DTN/N-CDs probe bound with cDNA to immobilize on the AuE surface, and the AuE was immersed in the phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4) solution containing K2S2O8 to produce ECL signal, which was recorded for highly sensitive detection of AFB1, realizing a low detection limit of 77 pg/mL. The superiority and reliability of the developed ECL aptasensor was confirmed in actual Ciox seed samples in a simple and straightforward way, avoiding the environmental adaptation vulnerability of complex structures and matrix interferences, providing a versatile tool for more trace component analysis in food safety and environment monitoring fields.

Citric acid (99.5%, CAS No.: 77–92–9), Urea (CAS No.: 57–13–6), Tris-(2-carboxyrthyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP, 98%, CAS No.: 51805–45–9) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH, 98%, CAS No.: 1633–78–9) was obtained from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) (K3[Fe(CN)6], 99.5%, CAS No.: 13746–66–2), and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, 99.5%, CAS No.: 7727–21–1) were collected from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1, CAS No.: 1162–65–8), ochratoxin A (OTA, CAS No.: 303–47–9), deoxynivalenol (DON, CAS No.: 51481–10–8), and zearalenone (ZEN, CAS No.: 17924–92–4) standards were obtained from Pribolab Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Tris-EDTA buffer solution (TE buffer, pH 8.0), Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 8.0), and phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) were got from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Magnesium chloride (MgCl2, CAS No.: 7786–30–3) was supplied by from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ·cm) was applied throughout the experiments.

The four sequence strands of the DTN were listed as follows (from 5′ to 3′):
Strand 1 of the DTN: NH2-(CH2)6-ACATTCCTAAGTCTGAAACATTACAGCTTGCTACACGAGAAGAGCCGCCATAGTATTTTTCACGTGTTGTCTCTCTGTGTCTCGTG.
Strand 2 of the DTN: NH2-(CH2)6-TATCACCAGGCAGTTGACAGTGTAGCAAGCTGTAATAGATGCGAGGGTCCAATAC.
Strand 3 of the DTN: NH2-(CH2)6-TCAACTGCCTGGTGATAAAACGACACTACGTGGGAATCTACTATGGCGGCTCTTC.
Strand 4 of the DTN: NH2-(CH2)6-TTCAGACTTAGGAATGTGCTTCCCACGTAGTGTCGTTTGTATTGGACCCTCGCAT.

All the ECL measurements were performed on an MPI-E Ⅱ ECL working station (Xi’an Remex Electronic Science and Technology Co. Ltd., Xi’an, China). EC signals were recorded on a CHI660E EC working station (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Corporation, China). The three-electrode system was consisted of working Au electrode (AuE, diameter in 2 mm), saturated calomel reference electrode and platinum wire auxiliary electrode. All ECL and EC experiments were carried out at room temperature. Validation of AFB1 in positive Coix seed samples was realized on an ultra-fast liquid chromatography (UFLC, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) system coupled with the Applied Bio-system Sciex 5500 QTRAP® hybrid triple quadrupole/linear ion trap mass spectrometer (Applied Biosystems/MDS Sciex, Foster City, CA, USA) equipped with the electrospray ionization (ESI) source. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained in virtue of a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 (FEI, Hillsboro, Oregon, USA) at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientic, USA).

Nine batches of Coix seed samples (CS1-CS9) were collected from different regions in China, and the Netherlands. These samples were used for recovery tests to validate the feasibility of the developed ECL aptasensor, as well as for AFB1 detection in actual samples.

First, a batch of blank (AFB1-free) Coix seed sample confirmed by UFLC-MS/MS method was ground into powder and passed through a 24-mesh sieve, followed by the addition of different concentrations of AFB1 standard solution. Then, 1 g of the spiked sample powder was dissolved in an 80% methanol solvent system. The mixture was treated by vortexing for 5 min and ultrasonication for 10 min for the extraction of AFB1, followed by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and passed through a 0.22-μm syringe filter to remove the matrix interfering substances. Finally, the filtrate was diluted with a 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.4) solution at a 1:4 (v/v) ratio for recovery tests.

For actual sample analysis, all the nine batches of Coix seeds were ground into powder and passed through a 24-mesh sieve. 1 g of the sample powder was dissolved in 10 mL of 80% methanol, followed by the above-mentioned procedure for AFB1 detection by using the fabricated ECL aptasensor.

The synthesis of N-doped CDs (N-CDs) was referred to the reported method [28] after minor modifications. 1.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixed solution was then transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, and heated in an oven at 160 ℃ for 4 h. After sufficient reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000) for 48 h to remove the small molecule impurities. The resulted N-CDs solution was placed at 4 °C for further use.

The DTN was synthesized based on the modified technique [29]. In brief, equimolar quantities of four strands were dissolved in TE buffer (10 mM Tris, 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) solution to a final concentration of 10 μM, respectively. Four strand (10 μL of each strand) solutions were mixed with 10 μL of TM buffer (20 mM Tris, 50 mM MgCl2, pH 8.0). The mixed solution was heated to 95 °C for 10 min, then cooled to 60 °C for 10 min, and finally cooled to 4 ℃ for more than 30 min. The obtained DTN solution was stored at 4 ℃ prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-CDs and DTN based ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: First, the Au electrode was polished with 0.3 µm and 0.05 µm alumina slurry, respectively, to a mirror face. Subsequently, it was soaked in Piranha solution for 10 min, and then was washed by ultrapure water and dried with a nitrogen stream.

Approximately 20 μL of the activated cDNA (1.5 μM, pretreated with 5 mM TCEP for 80 min to shear the disulfide bonds) solution was dropped onto the AuE surface and incubated at 37 ℃ for 1.5 h to assemble a cDNA layer. The electrode was then immersed into 1 mM MCH for 1 h at room temperature to block the non-specific binding sites. Afterwards, 20 μL of 3 μM AFB1 aptamer solution was dropped onto the AuE surface and incubated for 1 h at 37 ℃ for hybridization with cDNA. Then, the modified AuE was incubated with different concentrations of AFB1 for 1 h, which would be identified and competitively bounded with the aptamers. The AFB1-aptamer complex was afterwards dissociated from the electrode, leading to the exposure of the cDNA again. Under the catalysis of EDC and NHS, the DTN was bound with N-CDs, and 20 μL of the DTN/N-CDs was dropped onto the AuE surface to hybridize with cDNA for 1 h. Finally, the N-CDs and DTN based ECL aptasensor was fabricated for AFB1 detection in actual samples. All the incubation processes were performed in the high-humidity environment. After each step of incubation, the modified AuE surface was washed with PBS to avoid the non-specific binding, and dried under nitrogen flow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL measurement of AFB1 in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL response of the aptasensor was measured in 15 mL of 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. The Au electrode modified with cDNA/MCH/DTN-N-CDs probe was used as working electrode, and the platinum wire and saturated calomel were used as auxiliary and reference electrodes, respectively. The three electrodes were scanned by using a cyclic voltammetry (CV) method in the potential range of − 0.6 ∼ − 1.6 V at a scanning rate of 0.1 V/s, and the photomultiplier tube was set at 800 V.

For AFB1 detection in standard or real sample solutions, the cDNA/MCH/Apt modified Au electrode was incubated with 50 μL of standard or Coix seed sample solution at 37 ℃, followed by incubating with DTN/N-CDs probe. The ECL response intensity was recorded by using an MPI-E Ⅱ system for quantitation of AFB1.

Here, citric acid and urea were regarded as the precursor molecules for the hydrothermal synthesis of N-CDs in ultra-pure water, which exhibited strong blue fluorescence. Then, the structural feature of N-CDs was studied by TEM. The image in Fig. 1A showed that the synthesized blue-emissive N-CDs presented a homogeneous dispersal state in water with a clear-cut globular structure. The inserted picture in Fig. 1A illustrated that the sizes of N-CDs were mainly in the range of 1.4–3.6 nm with an average size of 2.35 nm. Furthermore, the surface functional groups and chemical bonds of N-CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectrum. Fig. 1B indicated that the peak at 3421 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds, and the peak at 3207 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CC-H. While, the peak at 1702 cm−1 was in accordance with the vibration of CO bond, and two characteristic peaks at 1577 cm−1 and 1419 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO-, respectively. The results revealed that the N-CDs were functionalized with plentiful hydrophilic groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which endowed the synthesized N-CDs with favorable water solubility.

Agarose gel electrophoresis technique was utilized to confirm the formation of DTN. The electrophoresis rate of substances with large molecular weight was slowed down, leading to a shorter electrophoretic shift. As displayed in Fig. 1 C, lane 1 referred to the DNA marker, and lane 2 at 55 bp showed the basic base of strand 1, respectively. Lanes 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 loaded the mixtures from parallel experiments of DTN synthesis, which exhibited a slower migration band than lane 2 and approximately the same electrophoretic shift among them. Such results could be assigned to the successful formation of DTN [30].
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective method for characterizing the properties of modified Au electrode surface regarding the step-by-step construction of the aptasensor.

As shown in Fig. 2A, the radius of the semicircle part in the EIS of the bare AuE was extremely small, indicating the good conductivity of the Au electrode. After modifying with cDNA, the radius of the semicircle part in the EIS corresponding to the charge transfer resistance exhibited apparent change and was bigger than that of the bare AuE. MCH that could block the non-specific active sites on the AuE surface further increased charge transfer resistance due to blocking electron transport. After the AFB1 aptamer was introduced to hybridize with cDNA, the impedance was further highlighted due to the poor conductivity of the aptamer-cDNA complex. While, upon the presence of target AFB1, it was specially identified and competitively captured by the corresponding aptamer, leading to the dissociation of AFB1 aptamer from the electrode surface and the exposure of cDNA, as well as the decreased radius of the semicircle part and increased conductivity. In addition, the impedance further increased because of the repulsion between negatively charged phosphate backbones of DTN and [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, which greatly hindered the access of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- towards the electrode. All these findings confirmed the successful step-by-step construction of the proposed N-CDs and DTN based ECL aptasenor and further feasibility for AFB1 detection.

Moreover, the biosensor was characterized through ECL experiments (Fig. 2B). No obvious ECL signal was observed for AuE-cDNA, AuE-cDNA-MCH, AuE-cDNA-MCH-Apt, AuE-cDNA-MCH-Apt-AFB1 (1.0 ×103 ng/mL) (black curve). After the addition of DTN/N-CDs nanostructure, an outstanding ECL signal was measured (red curve), demonstrating the successful introduction of the signal probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fabrication conditions for the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions such as the concentration of cDNA, the incubation time of cDNA and the pH of the co-reaction solution were optimized to obtain optimal performance.

Various concentrations of cDNA solutions were prepared to incubate with AuE to test the signal with the presence of 10 μg/mL AFB1 in order to optimize the surface density of the cDNA on the AuE. As shown in Fig. 3A, the ECL intensity (I, a.u.) increased with the increment of cDNA concentration from 0.5 to 1.5 μM, but declined when the concentration of cDNA further increased. Therefore, 1.5 μM of cDNA was employed for constructing the ECL aptasensor.

Besides, the incubation time of cDNA was also a vital factor that influenced the combination of the cDNA on AuE surface, which was evaluated through investigating the ECL intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the I value of the N-CDs nearly reached a plateau when cDNA was incubated on AuE for 1.5 h. Thus, 1.5 h was applied as the optimized incubation time of cDNA.

The ECL efficiency of N-CDs was closely related to pH of the co-reaction system, which increased with the increment of pH from 5 to 7.4, but lowered when the pH exceeded 7.4 (Fig. 3C). Thus, the optimal pH of the co-reaction solution was set at 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ECL sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The assay performance of this newly-developed aptasensor was evaluated by measuring the ECL responses at various concentrations of AFB1. Under the optimal conditions, different concentrations (1.0 ×10−1-1.0 ×104 ng/mL) of AFB1 standard solutions were dropped onto the AuE surface to competitively bind with the aptamers. And the exposed cDNA would hybridize with the DTN/N-CDs nanostructure, accompanying with the ECL signal output.

Fig. 4A showed that the ECL signals increased proportionally with increasing the added concentration of AFB1, presenting a positive relationship. A good linear equation between I and the logarithm of AFB1 concentration (1.0 ×10−1-1.0 ×104 ng/mL) was obtained as I = 1012.442 lgC + 4677.019 with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9959 (Fig. 4B). The dictation ability was high with a limit of detection (LOD) low to 77 pg/mL verified the high detection ability of the developed ECL aptasensor. Compared with other methods in Table 1 [9,10,17,31–35], the newly-developed ECL aptasensor with excellent analytical performance including wide linear range and low LOD could realize ultra-sensitive detection of AFB1 in real samples.

In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability or specificity of the ECL aptasensor, the ECL intensity in the presence of AFB1 (1.0 ×103 ng/mL) and other three mycotoxin including OTA, ZEN, and DON at 1.0 × 104 ng/mL, respectively, was recorded under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4C, the addition of AFB1 caused a remarkably I value than that of other three interferences mycotoxins, revealing the high specificity and anti-interference ability of the ECL aptasensor for AFB1 detection.

To investigate the accuracy and practicability of the new aptasensor, low (0.1 ng/mL), medium (10 ng/mL), and high (1000 ng/mL) of AFB1 standard solutions were added into the actual blank (AFB1-free) Coix seed sample powder. The spiked Coix seed samples were treated according to the above-mentioned procedure, and the sample extractions were dropped on the modified AuE surface for AFB1 quantitation. Recovery (%) was determined in triplicate (n = 3) and calculated as the ratio of the measured amount by the proposed aptasensor to the actually added amount of AFB1. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries of AFB1 in the fortified Coix seed ranged from 89.54% to 111.0% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 11.5%, which was acceptable. These results verified that the constructed ECL aptasensor with fascinating accuracy and reliability could be introduced for AFB1 analysis in real Coix seed samples.

The reproducibility of the ECL aptasensor was assessed by scanning continuously the modified AuE for 11 cycles between − 0.6 and − 1.6 V to record the signal response responding to 10 ng/mL of AFB1. Fig. 4D showed that the measured I values did not present significant fluctuation with a low RSD of 2.70%, manifesting fascinating reproducibility and good operational reliability of the developed aptasensor.

Finally, the stability of the ECL aptasensor that was vital regarding its practical application was tested through a 7-day storage in a refrigerator at 4 ℃. ECL intensities were measured at the 1st, 2nd, 4th, and 7th day (Fig. 4E), which were almost same, indicating outstanding stability of the DTN/N-CDs based ECL aptasenor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application of the proposed ECL aptasensor for AFB1 detection
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability of the newly-developed DTN/N-CDs based ECL aptasensing platform, nine batches of actual Coix seed samples (CS1-CS9) from different origins were measured for AFB1. As illustrated in Table 3, one Coix seed sample (CS5) was detected with AFB1 at a concentration of 23.25 μg/kg, which largely exceeded the official maximum residue limit of 5 μg/kg [36]. This result was confirmed via a UFLC-MS/MS in the previous research by our team [37]. Another eight batches of Coix seed samples were confirmed to be AFB1-negative. Thus, the proposed ECL aptasensor with satisfactory specificity and accuracy, high sensitivity, and good reliability could be applied for AFB1 detection in complex Coix seed samples.

In this study, a highly-efficient N-CDs and DTN based competitive-type ECL aptasensor was successfully fabricated for the selective and ultra-sensitive detection of trace AFB1 in complex matrices. The nitrogen-doping strategy could remarkably enhance electron transfer between co-reactants and N-CDs, displaying improved quantum efficiency. The introduction of DTN as the carrier offered more sites for immobilizing abundant N-CDs to achieve the ECL signal amplification to significantly improve the detection sensitivity. By integrating DTN and N-CDs with superior ECL performance as the signal probes, the ECL aptasensor displayed excellent sensitivity with a low LOD of 77 pg/mL and a wide linear concentration range of 1.0 × 10−1-1.0 × 104 ng/mL for AFB1, as well as extreme selectivity, high reproducibility, good stability, and fascinating accuracy with acceptable recoveries of 89.54–111.0% in the spiked Coix seed matrices. Successful application of this ECL aptasensor in actual Coix seed samples from different origins confirmed its practicability in real-world analysis. The proposed ECL aptasensor is simple, sensitive, and accurate, and cost-effective, proposing a universal platform for more trace analysis in food safety and environmental monitoring by replacing the corresponding aptamers.

Ying Li: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Pengyue Song: Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Qingbin Xu: Investigation. Wenjuan Wu: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Nan Long: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jiabo Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Lidong Zhou: Data curation. Ruile Pan: Methodology, Investigation. Weijun Kong: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support from the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (7222285, 7232265), National Natural Science Foundation of China (82274089, 81973474), and the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources (SKLTCM2022022).

Food Res. Int.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ying Li is completing her Master degree at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College. The subject of her research focused on the development of novel electrochemiluminescence sensors.

Pengyue Song is completing her Master degree at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College. The subject of her research was on the development of novel sensors.

Qingbin Xu is completing her Master degree at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College. The subject of her research was on the development of novel electrochemical sensors.

Wenjuan Wu is completing her his Ph.D. at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College. The subject of her research was on the development of new methods for traditional Chinese medicines evaluation.

Nan Long has completing her Master degree at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College. The subject of her research was on the development of novel sensors.
Jiabo Wang completed his Ph.D. at Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine in 2008. He is a professor at School of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Capital Medical University.

Lidong Zhou completed his Ph.D. at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College in 1999. The subject of his research was on the analysis of traditional Chinese medicines.

Ruile Pan completed her Ph.D. at Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College in 2000. The subject of her research was on developing new methods for traditional Chinese medicines evaluation.

Weijun Kong completed his Ph.D. at Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine in 2011. He is a professor at School of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Capital Medical University. The subject of his research was on the development of new material-based sensing methods for trace pollutant detection.
